mn48:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Park.
mn48:10.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn48:10.2: ‘Endowed as I am with this kind of view, is there outside this any other ascetic or brahmin endowed with such a view?’
mn48:10.4: ‘Endowed as I am with this kind of view, there is outside this no other ascetic or brahmin endowed with such a view.’
mn48:10.5: This is the third knowledge he has attained: noble, transcendent, not shared with ordinary people.
mn48:11.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn48:11.10: having confessed it, revealed it, and laid it open, he undertakes restraint for the future.
mn48:11.12: ‘I too have the kind of nature that a person accomplished in view has.’
mn48:11.13: This is the fourth knowledge he has attained: noble, transcendent, not shared with ordinary people.
mn48:11.2: ‘I too have the kind of nature that a person accomplished in view has.’
mn48:11.3: And what kind of nature does a person accomplished in view have?
mn48:11.4: This is the nature of a person accomplished in view, *bhikkhus*:
mn48:11.5: even if he commits an offense of such a kind that a rehabilitation from that offense is recognized, still he quickly confesses it, reveals it, and lays it open before the Teacher or wise companions in the holy life;
mn48:11.6: having confessed it, revealed it, and laid it open, he undertakes restraint for the future.
mn48:11.7: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a small infant boy, helpless and lying on his back, if he steps on a live coal with his hand or foot, quickly pulls it back;
mn48:11.8: even so, *bhikkhus*, this is the nature of a person accomplished in view:
mn48:11.9: even if he commits an offense of such a kind that a rehabilitation from that offense is recognized, still he quickly confesses it, reveals it, and lays it open before the Teacher or wise companions in the holy life;
mn48:12.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn48:12.10: ‘I too have the kind of nature that a person accomplished in view has.’
mn48:12.11: This is the fifth knowledge he has attained: noble, transcendent, not shared with ordinary people.
mn48:12.2: ‘I too have the kind of nature that a person accomplished in view has.’
mn48:12.3: And what kind of nature does a person accomplished in view have?
mn48:12.4: This is the nature of a person accomplished in view, *bhikkhus*:
mn48:12.5: even though he may be engaged in the various duties to be done for companions in the holy life, still he has keen concern for the training in higher ethical conduct, the training in the higher mind [*adhicittasikkhāya*], and the training in higher discernment [*adhipaññāsikkhāya*].
mn48:12.6: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a cow with a young calf nibbles the grass while watching over her calf;
mn48:12.7: even so, *bhikkhus*, this is the nature of a person accomplished in view:
mn48:12.8: even though he may be engaged in the various duties to be done for companions in the holy life, still he has keen concern for the training in higher ethical conduct, the training in the higher mind [*adhicittasikkhāya*], and the training in higher discernment [*adhipaññāsikkhāya*].
mn48:13.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn48:13.2: ‘I too have the kind of strength that a person accomplished in view has.’
mn48:13.3: And what kind of strength does a person accomplished in view have?
mn48:13.4: This is the strength of a person accomplished in view, *bhikkhus*: when the *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*] proclaimed by the Tathāgata are being taught, he listens to the *dhamma*, making it paramount, attending to it, applying his whole mind, and giving ear.
mn48:13.6: ‘I too have the kind of strength that a person accomplished in view has.’
mn48:13.7: This is the sixth knowledge he has attained: noble, transcendent, not shared with ordinary people.
mn48:14.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn48:14.2: ‘I too have the kind of strength that a person accomplished in view has.’
mn48:14.3: And what kind of strength does a person accomplished in view have?
mn48:14.4: This is the strength of a person accomplished in view, *bhikkhus*: when the *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*] proclaimed by the Tathāgata are being taught, he gains inspiration in the meaning, gains inspiration in the *dhamma*, and gains gladness connected with the *dhamma*.
mn48:14.6: ‘I too have the kind of strength that a person accomplished in view has.’
mn48:14.7: This is the seventh knowledge he has attained: noble, transcendent, not shared with ordinary people.
mn48:15.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, for a noble disciple endowed with these seven factors, the nature is well established for the realization of the fruit of stream-entry.
mn48:15.2: Thus a noble disciple endowed with these seven factors is endowed with the fruit of stream-entry.”
mn48:15.4: Delighted, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn48:15.5: At Kosambī is finished, the eighth.
mn48:2.1: At that time, the *bhikkhus* at Kosambī had become quarrelsome, contentious, and disputatious, living while stabbing one another with verbal spears.
mn48:2.2: They neither made one another understand nor came to an understanding, neither reconciled one another nor came to reconciliation.
mn48:3.1: Then a certain *bhikkhu* went to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone to the *Bhagavā*, he paid homage to him and sat down to one side. Seated to one side, that *bhikkhu* said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn48:3.2: “Here, bhante, at Kosambī the *bhikkhus* have become quarrelsome, contentious, and disputatious, living while stabbing one another with verbal spears. They neither make one another understand nor come to an understanding, neither reconcile one another nor come to reconciliation.”
mn48:4.4: “Yes, bhante,” that *bhikkhu* replied to the *Bhagavā*. Then he went to those *bhikkhus*, and having gone to them, he said this:
mn48:4.6: “Yes, friend,” those *bhikkhus* replied to that *bhikkhu*. Then they went to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone to the *Bhagavā*, they paid homage to him and sat down to one side. When those *bhikkhus* were seated to one side, the *Bhagavā* said this to them:
mn48:4.7: “Is it really true, *bhikkhus*, that you have become quarrelsome, contentious, and disputatious, living while stabbing one another with verbal spears,
mn48:4.8: and that you neither make one another understand nor come to an understanding, neither reconcile one another nor come to reconciliation?”
mn48:5.2: at the time when you have become quarrelsome, contentious, and disputatious, living while stabbing one another with verbal spears, is bodily action of *mettā* [*mettaṁ kāyakammaṁ*] present toward companions in the holy life, openly and privately, verbal action of *mettā* … mental action of *mettā* [*mettaṁ manokammaṁ*] present toward companions in the holy life, openly and privately?”
mn48:5.4: “So then, *bhikkhus*, at the time when you have become quarrelsome, contentious, and disputatious, living while stabbing one another with verbal spears, neither is bodily action of *mettā* [*mettaṁ kāyakammaṁ*] present toward companions in the holy life, openly and privately, nor verbal action of *mettā* … nor mental action of *mettā* [*mettaṁ manokammaṁ*] present toward companions in the holy life, openly and privately.
mn48:5.5: Then what is it, foolish men, knowing what and seeing what, that you have become quarrelsome, contentious, and disputatious, living while stabbing one another with verbal spears, and that you neither make one another understand nor come to an understanding, neither reconcile one another nor come to reconciliation?
mn48:5.6: That will be for your harm and suffering for a long time, foolish men.”
mn48:6.10: Again, *bhikkhus*, whatever gains a *bhikkhu* gets that are lawful and lawfully obtained, even if it is no more than what is in his bowl, he is one who does not enjoy such gains without sharing them, but uses them in common with virtuous companions in the holy life.
mn48:6.11: This too is a dhamma to be remembered, making for affection, making for respect, and leading to cohesion, to non-dispute, to concord, to unity.
mn48:6.12: Again, *bhikkhus*, those ethical qualities that are unbroken, untorn, unblemished, unspotted, freeing, praised by the discerning, not clung to, and leading to *samādhi* [*samādhisaṁvattanikāni*] — in such ethical qualities a *bhikkhu* dwells sharing ethical conduct in common with companions in the holy life, openly and privately.
mn48:6.13: This too is a dhamma to be remembered, making for affection, making for respect, and leading to cohesion, to non-dispute, to concord, to unity.
mn48:6.14: Again, *bhikkhus*, that view which is noble, emancipating, and leads one who acts in accordance with it rightly to the ending of *dukkha* — with such a view a *bhikkhu* dwells sharing view in common with companions in the holy life, openly and privately.
mn48:6.15: This too is a dhamma to be remembered, making for affection, making for respect, and leading to cohesion, to non-dispute, to concord, to unity.
mn48:6.16: These, *bhikkhus*, are the six dhammas that are to be remembered, make for affection, make for respect, and lead to cohesion, to non-dispute, to concord, to unity.
mn48:6.2: “These six dhammas, *bhikkhus*, are to be remembered, make for affection, make for respect, and lead to cohesion, to non-dispute, to concord, to unity.
mn48:6.4: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* has bodily action of *mettā* [*mettaṁ kāyakammaṁ*] toward companions in the holy life, openly and privately.
mn48:6.5: This too is a dhamma to be remembered, making for affection, making for respect, and leading to cohesion, to non-dispute, to concord, to unity.
mn48:6.6: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* has verbal action of *mettā* [*mettaṁ vacīkammaṁ*] toward companions in the holy life, openly and privately.
mn48:6.7: This too is a dhamma to be remembered, making for affection, making for respect, and leading to cohesion, to non-dispute, to concord, to unity.
mn48:6.8: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* has mental action of *mettā* [*mettaṁ manokammaṁ*] toward companions in the holy life, openly and privately.
mn48:6.9: This too is a dhamma to be remembered, making for affection, making for respect, and leading to cohesion, to non-dispute, to concord, to unity.
mn48:7.1: Of these six dhammas to be remembered, *bhikkhus*, this is the foremost, the unifying one, the binding one — namely, that view which is noble, emancipating, and leads one who acts in accordance with it rightly to the ending of *dukkha*.
mn48:7.2: Just as, *bhikkhus*, in a gabled house the roof peak is the top, the unifying point, the binding point — namely, the roof peak;
mn48:7.3: even so, *bhikkhus*, of these six dhammas to be remembered, this is the foremost, the unifying one, the binding one — namely, that view which is noble, emancipating, and leads one who acts in accordance with it rightly to the ending of *dukkha*.
mn48:8.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, is that view noble, emancipating, and leading one who acts in accordance with it rightly to the ending of *dukkha*?
mn48:8.10: If, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is preoccupied with thoughts of the next world, his mind is indeed obsessed [*pariyuṭṭhitacitto*].
mn48:8.11: If, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* has become quarrelsome, contentious, and disputatious, living while stabbing others with verbal spears, his mind is indeed obsessed [*pariyuṭṭhitacitto*].
mn48:8.13: ‘There is in me no such internal obsession not abandoned, such that, with my mind obsessed [*pariyuṭṭhitacitto*] by that obsession, I would not know and see as it truly is.
mn48:8.14: My mind is well directed toward awakening to the truths.’
mn48:8.15: This is the first knowledge he has attained: noble, transcendent, not shared with ordinary people.
mn48:8.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the forest or to the root of a tree or to an empty hut, reflects thus:
mn48:8.3: ‘Is there in me any internal obsession not abandoned, such that, with my mind obsessed [*pariyuṭṭhitacitto*] by that obsession, I would not know and see as it truly is?’
mn48:8.4: If, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is obsessed by sensual desire, his mind is indeed obsessed [*pariyuṭṭhitacitto*].
mn48:8.5: If, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is obsessed by ill will, his mind is indeed obsessed [*pariyuṭṭhitacitto*].
mn48:8.6: If, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is obsessed by sloth and torpor, his mind is indeed obsessed [*pariyuṭṭhitacitto*].
mn48:8.7: If, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is obsessed by restlessness and remorse, his mind is indeed obsessed [*pariyuṭṭhitacitto*].
mn48:8.8: If, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is obsessed by doubt, his mind is indeed obsessed [*pariyuṭṭhitacitto*].
mn48:8.9: If, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is preoccupied with thoughts of this world, his mind is indeed obsessed [*pariyuṭṭhitacitto*].
mn48:9.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn48:9.2: ‘As I cultivate, develop, and make much of this view, do I gain settling [*samathaṁ*] for myself, do I gain quenching for myself?’
mn48:9.4: ‘As I cultivate, develop, and make much of this view, I gain settling [*samathaṁ*] for myself, I gain quenching for myself.’
mn48:9.5: This is the second knowledge he has attained: noble, transcendent, not shared with ordinary people.
